title: Making mammalian cells ‘photosynthetic’ could treat dry eye
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Mapping Earth’s magnetic field, the grisly fate of movie geologists, and more
Brought to you by Science & SciLifeLab Prize for Young Scientists 
View this email in your browser
18 May 2026
You’d better saddle up for today’s Deep Dive, which explores the origin of horseback riding. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including research that puts the “eyes” in “photosynthesize” and the various ways geologists have died in movies.
SPACE  |  ScienceINSIDER
Spacecraft will soon map Earth’s shape-shifting magnetic field
A new Chinese-European spacecraft is about to give scientists something they’ve never really had before: a wide-angle view of Earth’s magnetic shield as it gets pummeled by the solar wind.

The mission, called SMILE, is scheduled to launch May 19 from French Guiana. Its goal is to watch how streams of charged particles from the Sun buffet and reshape the magnetosphere—the invisible magnetic bubble that helps protect Earth from harmful radiation.

Previous missions have probed the magnetosphere from the inside. SMILE, however, will back way off, climbing more than 120,000 kilometers above the North Pole so it can image the whole system at once. Its main instrument detects x-rays produced where solar particles collide with atoms far away from Earth, effectively tracing the constantly shifting boundary between the solar wind and the magnetosphere.

Researchers hope the mission will improve forecasts of solar storms, which can disrupt satellites, radio communications, and even power grids. SMILE will also track ultraviolet auroras, helping scientists follow solar particles as they funnel into Earth’s atmosphere near the poles.
Read the full story
bioengineering  |  news from Science
Making eyes ‘photosynthetic’ could treat common vision problem
New research shows that these disk-shaped photosynthesis centers, known as thylakoids, can work in mammalian eyes. Scientists behind the work isolated the structures from store-bought spinach.  jeremy burgess/science source
Over the years, science fiction writers have penned many a tale about photosynthetic humans, some even complete with greenish skin. After all, the idea of harvesting physiological energy from sunlight is obviously highly appealing. And although no animals are naturally capable of photosynthesis, scientists are attempting to bestow the skill on some.

Now, one research team has taken some of the necessary molecular machinery out of spinach cells and introduced it into mouse and human eye cells—including into the eyes of live mice. This approach, the group reported in Cell, could one day treat a common, painful condition called dry eye. The strategy, dubbed LEAF, not only spurred cells to produce a molecule that can soothe inflammation but also reversed dry eye damage in the mice. Questions remain about the safety of making an animal’s cells use even this limited form of photosynthesis, but the concept has captivated some scientists.

Choosing to target the eye for initial tests of LEAF was shrewd, says molecular biologist Shahaf Peleg, who wasn’t involved in the new work: “It’s such an elegant solution. All you need to do is stare at the light, which you do anyway.”
Read the Full Story
scientific community  |  careers
Movie geologists are lucky if they live to see the sequel
As an asteroid hurtles toward Earth in Armageddon (1998), a geologist teams up with a veteran oil rig team on a desperate mission: land on the space rock, drill into its surface, and detonate a nuclear bomb deep inside. They succeed, but the geologist, played by Steve Buscemi, is one of few who make it home alive.

According to a new study of movie geologists, Armageddon’s hero was one of the lucky ones. Of 202 movie geologists found in 141 movies released between 1919 and 2023, 69 die onscreen or are found dead—a mortality rate of about one third, according to a paper published last month in Geology Today. The authors, several geologist movie buffs, began their analysis more than a decade ago and have provided regular updates, although this is the first in a scientific journal.

Murder was found to be the leading cause of the fictional geologists’ deaths, responsible for 30 of the fatalities. Geological hazards, including falling into a crater and drowning in quicksand, accounted for another 12 deaths, tied with those involving aliens—the massive worms in the Tremors movies are thought to have come from off planet although the star geologists survive in those flicks. In a one-off case, a geologist in Walkabout (1971) commits suicide after reading a textbook in the Australian outback. The authors note that they “hope this does not provoke demands for warning texts on structural geology textbooks!”
Read the Full Story
SPONSORED
Rethinking aging outcomes through immune system biology
Immune aging shapes inflammation, resilience, and decline—but how should we measure its impact? Join Andrea Maier and Mitsuo Maruyama as they explore immunosenescence, intrinsic capacity, and emerging links between immune function, gut biology, and healthspan.
Register now
deep dive
This skull found in Germany and dating back to roughly between 3400 and 3100 B.C.E. belonged to a horse with tobiano spotting—a color pattern only found in domesticated horses.  torsten schunke, hauke arnold, and jurai lipták/all state office for heritage management and archaeology saxony-anhalt, germany
The history of horseback riding gets even longer
The relationship between humans and horses has spanned continents and civilizations, shaping history in profound ways. Equestrian culture helped forge global trade networks, revolutionized both agriculture and warfare, and played a crucial role in the rise—and fall—of empires.

Scholars continue to debate when this historic bond actually began. Some scientists have argued, for example, that horse domestication first occurred about 2200 to 2100 B.C.E., around the same time humans invented chariots. This time period marked the expansion of horses from a lineage known as DOM2, the direct genetic ancestors of all modern domesticated horses. These equines possessed favorable genetic mutations linked to endurance and calmness around humans—and they eventually galloped their way across Europe, Anatolia, the Near East, and Central Asia. But research published last week in Science Advances challenges this idea, suggesting that humans began riding horses much earlier than previously thought.

Researchers combined archaeological artifacts, ancient DNA, bone studies, and other evidence to show that horses from multiple genetic backgrounds were being eaten, managed, milked, and ridden in communities across Eurasia long before 2200 B.C.E. The team identified people from the Yamnaya culture, who lived across the Eurasian steppes between about 3200 and 2600 B.C.E., as early riders of DOM2 horses. “Rather than a sudden breakthrough, domestication was a protracted, regionally varied process whose transformative effects on human mobility and social organization began as early as the fourth, if not the fifth millennium B.C.E.,” the study authors write.

It also appears that, centuries before the DOM2 lineage became widespread, humans were already selectively breeding horses for particular traits. One horse skull found in Germany and dating back to roughly between 3400 and 3100 B.C.E. carried the genetic signature for tobiano spotting, a color pattern found only in domesticated horses.
Read the Paper
Et Cetera
Mouse, mislabeled
A survey of more than 300 strains of laboratory mice has revealed widespread mismatches between reported name and actual genetic makeup, potentially compromising the reproducibility and interpretation of mouse studies. “This study is another wake-up call for biomedical research,” said one immunologist. “If we don’t fully understand the genetics of the mice we’re using, we risk misinterpreting how diseases actually work.”
Science Policy article  |  Read more at Nature
A current dilemma
The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), a vital system of ocean currents, is already substantially weakened because of climate change. Now, new research suggests attempts to clean up air pollution could destabilize it even further.
Environmental Research: Climate Paper  |  Read more at New Scientist
Epic burial
Scientists have unearthed a 2000-year-old Egyptian mummy who was buried with a remarkable bit of reading material: a papyrus fragment featuring lines from Homer’s “Iliad.”
Read more at The New York Times
Leadership, I now see, is not dominating the room or trying to be everywhere at once, but creating a space where others can become stronger, more confident, and more responsible than they believed possible.
WORKING LIFE  |  14 May 2026  |  João Conde
Last but not least
It’s only natural for scientists to want to preserve wildlife, but can their methods sometimes go too far? As science journalist Humberto Basilio writes in his thrilling feature story, one herpetologist in Ecuador may have crossed the line.
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Eric Hand, John Travis, and Perri Thaler

began riding horses much earlier than previously thought
